summary: Conditional relay of caller traffic to third-party Render endpoint plus hardcoded default API key
details: common-tg-service@1.3.222 ships two concerning runtime behaviors that activate when a consuming NestJS app mounts and uses the library, not at install or import time. (1) dist/utils/fetchWithTimeout.js hardcodes https://helper-thge.onrender.com/execute-request as a fallback destination: when an upstream HTTP call returns 403 or 495 and the consumer has not set process.env.bypassURL, the utility POSTs the original request — including the target URL, headers (which may carry consumer-supplied auth tokens), and body — to that Render-hosted endpoint controlled by a third party. (2) dist/guards/auth.guard.js accepts the static string 'santoor' as a valid API key whenever process.env.X_API_KEY is unset, and AppController exposes a generic /execute-request HTTP proxy guarded by that AuthGuard, enabling SSRF / arbitrary-traffic relay through any deployment that mounts AppModule without overriding the default. Neither path executes on `npm install` or on `require()`; both require the consumer to wire the library into a running server. The relay endpoint and the default-key + open-proxy combination together create a meaningful operational risk for downstream consumers, and the relay shape (caller traffic and headers silently forwarded to an author-controlled host on error) overlaps with the silent-relay threat class enough to warrant human review.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
